questions.stem_preview: Anchoring bias is best described as the tendency to
questions.objective_ref: LO1 — Define anchoring bias in plain language and identify its two core components: the initial anchor and the adjustment heuristic

questions.stem_preview: Which two factors, working together, best explain why people stop adjusting too soon
questions.objective_ref: LO2 — Explain why the adjustment heuristic consistently produces insufficient adjustment — why people stop adjusting too soon

questions.stem_preview: In the Tversky and Kahneman wheel-of-fortune experiment, participants who saw the wheel land on 65
questions.objective_ref: LO3 — Recall the original Tversky and Kahneman wheel-of-fortune experiment as empirical evidence that anchoring bias is real, measurable, and independent of the relevance of the anchor

questions.stem_preview: A project manager asks an AI tool to estimate a software project budget
questions.objective_ref: LO4 — Identify at least three ways anchoring bias appears in AI-assisted work, including first-pass AI outputs acting as anchors

questions.stem_preview: A colleague says: 'Both anchoring bias and confirmation bias are cognitive biases, so they must work the same way'
questions.objective_ref: LO5 — Distinguish anchoring bias from confirmation bias by describing the mechanism that drives each one

questions.stem_preview: You are about to ask an AI assistant to suggest a project timeline
questions.objective_ref: LO6 — Apply awareness of anchoring bias to a self-check question: 'What number or starting point did I see first, and am I still too close to it?'
